role_summary: Marvell, Phillips County, Arkansas community organizer; mother of nine; collaborator with SNCC fieldworkers Howard Himmelbaum and Myrtle Glascoe (catalog entry 137) in 1960s Arkansas civil rights organizing; later Head Start program worker and founder of a Marvell community center.
ai_reading: Newsome Jackson's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17) at a cosine count of 157; the broader cluster includes Myrtle Gonza Glascoe (entry 137, the SNCC field secretary she collaborated with in Phillips County), Rosie Head (entry 111), and James Oscar Jones (entry 57). The Glascoe pairing is the catalog's clearest local-community-organizer-and-SNCC-field-secretary collaboration cosine: Newsome Jackson was the resident community partner who hosted Glascoe and Himmelbaum in their Phillips County voter-registration work, and the embedding catches that local-partner register through both their testimonies. The novel observation her page surfaces is the maternal-organizer-with-nine-children signature: Newsome Jackson's nine children meant her civil rights organizing happened from a position of substantial domestic responsibility, the catalog's clearest case of a maternal-community-anchor organizing role.
biographical_paragraph: While Newsome Jackson's LoC oral history carries an ingestion-only audit tier (the catalog ingested the transcript but did not subject it to the audit cascade, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), the external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid for entry 136 and the Civil Rights Digital Library [src: 1, src: 2]. Newsome Jackson was born in 1923 in Madison, Illinois; she married Earliss Jackson in 1943 and they had nine children. She settled in Marvell, Phillips County, Arkansas, where she became a community organizer; her early local-organizing work included rallying her community to renovate a segregated school. She assisted SNCC field secretaries Howard Himmelbaum and Myrtle Glascoe (catalog entry 137) in their Phillips County 1960s voter-registration and community-organizing work [src: 2]. After the most-photographed civil rights years she worked for the federal Head Start program and helped found a Marvell community center, the institutional legacy of her decades of local organizing [src: 2].

interview_snippets.speaker: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Newsome Jackson describes how the Marvell school boycott began, after repeated trips to the school board over the sewage backing up in the Black school produced no repair. She recounts what her husband said on the drive home from the last meeting:
interview_snippets.quote: my husband said, "If I could get these people to take the children out of school, they'd fix it." That man got up the next morning and went all through the community and was telling them about it, and everybody agreed to take their children out, and so that's the first boycott

interview_snippets.speaker: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked how she explained the Little Rock school-integration crisis to her children, Newsome Jackson connected it to the segregated schools around Marvell:
interview_snippets.quote: I just told them it's just like it is here in Marvell- whites go to one school and so now they're trying to integrate them and let blacks go to the school, and the fight was about because they didn't want them in their school and that's why they had to bring down the troops and all these things.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Newsome Jackson responds to a question about cross burnings, a shop fire, sabotage of the family equipment, and a shot fired at her car, naming what she understood those acts to mean:
interview_snippets.quote: I knew they were letting me know they didn't like what I was doing, and then I knew I wasn't going to stop doing what I was doing, and that's about it.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gertrude Newsome Jackson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Newsome Jackson describes the moment the idea for a community center for children came to her while she was working for Mid-Delta Community Service:
interview_snippets.quote: I was sitting at my desk and it just dropped in by spirit and all it said was something for children and it stayed there like, like a couple months, just something for children, just something within

interview_snippets.speaker: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
interview_snippets.lead_in: SNCC field secretary Myrtle Gonza Glascoe (entry 137), who organized in Phillips County, describes the Jackson family after sugar was put in the gas tank of their cotton picker, and characterizes Gertrude Jackson directly:
interview_snippets.quote: And Mr. Earlis Jackson and Mrs. Gertrude Jackson had 11 children and they were just practically fearless. And Mrs. Jackson had this kind of soft spoken way and she was easy going and I mean as I've gotten to know her since then, in many ways that was just a façade because she is powerful.

interview_snippets.speaker: Myrtle Gonza Glascoe
interview_snippets.lead_in: Glascoe credits Newsome Jackson with the organizing that built the Marvell community center, the institution Newsome Jackson describes founding in her own testimony:
interview_snippets.quote: But in the long run it was Mrs. Jackson who pulled people together and got them organized and they actually set up a community center and it still is in existence. It's limping, but it's still there.

gallery.alt: Arkansas Highway 1 in Marvell, Phillips County, Arkansas
gallery.caption: Arkansas Highway 1 in Marvell, Phillips County, Arkansas, south of US Route 49. Marvell is where Newsome Jackson settled, organized the school boycott over the sewage backing up in the Black school, and helped found the Boys Girls Adults Community Development Center; in her interview she notes that after the highway came through, the center lost most of its parking. Photographed by Wikimedia Commons contributor Brandonrush.
